speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me when I was about five or six years old, taken in a resort called Druzgieniki, D-R-U-Z-G-I-E-N-I-K-I. Here I am about 11 and 1/2 years old. This is 1939. And I know that exactly, because the-- the last two pictures that you saw survived because I had sent them to my aunt in Palestine. And she gave them to me after the war. This was in a-- in a resort in the summer of 1939. We are jumping now, I'm in my 20s in United States, no pictures having survived the war, or having been taken during the war. And I think I'm a student or maybe just after college. And this was, I think, a black and white photo that had been professionally colored. This is my glamour picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: January 16, 1955. That's my husband and I, our wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Samuels' name is-- his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bertram J. Samuels.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lovely couple. You know, I don't think we gave--

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my two sons, probably ages six and three, so I would say, taken in about 1963. On the right, the older, Mark Samuels, who is now a molecular biologist. And on the left, Samuel David Ross Samuels, who is now getting his doctorate at the University of Iowa. And he's married and has a little girl, whose picture you will see at the end. This is a picture at our older son's bar mitzvah, so it will be 1960-- '70, excuse me. And this is my husband, Bert, and myself at the bar mitzvah party. This is still the same bar mitzvah, a picture of my father, who was still alive and died a year or two later after this, doing the bracha over the challah. My father, Moses Salamon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-S-E-S, first name, S-A-L-A-M-O-N. And this is sometime in the late '80s, my husband, Bert, and myself during a dancing-- ballroom dancing competition.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do in that one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- I think we came in first or second. And from the looks of it, we're doing either the rumba or the cha-cha. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And these are some of our ballroom dancing trophies, the different prizes that we won over the years. I had many students over the years who participated successfully in various competitions. [? Abbot ?] [? Wang ?] is one of them, and this is in 1992. He had just won, I think, first or second place in physics in the state of New Jersey, one of my students, of course. This is my grad-- my retirement-- I want to say graduation. It is a graduation. A retirement picture, I'm retiring from Livingston High School after 26 years of teaching. And, of course, there was a party that we were given. And this is a picture of me and my husband, Bert. These are my two sons. Look what happened to those two beautiful children. On the right is Mark Samuels, the molecular biologist. On the left is Sam Samuels, who is now doing a doctorate in communications at the University of Iowa, Iowa City. This is my granddaughter, Miranda Hooper Samuels, who is now almost five-- in this picture, around four. And this is when they're already living in Iowa City, Iowa. And she was between 3 and 1/2 and four in this picture. This is, again, my granddaughter, Miranda, who was recently in a Hallmark-- a very small part had in a Hallmark Hall of Fame television movie. She's here with Patty Duke, who was the-- the main actress in the story. The story was called And, obviously, as you can see, they're playing Amish people. And this was aired, as a matter of fact, recently on April 21, 1996. She's very proud of her part and calls it 'my movie.' Well, that's the-- the picture of the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klara, I see that sitting next to you is a gentleman. Would you please introduce him to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband of 42 years, Bert Samuels, and my best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Bert, would you like to tell us anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you've heard a little bit about-- or a lot about Klara's early years and the tragedy. I might say that, in the United States, in my generation, I think we lived in the best times in history, probably better than there will ever be again. And I hope I have helped contribute some of this to my wife, Klara, during her latter part of her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has being married to a Holocaust survivor meant to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it made me feel probably more Jewish than I would have otherwise. I'm Jewish. I'm not a highly religious person. But in marrying Klara, I obtained an identity as being a Jew that I probably wouldn't have otherwise had. And I think that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you have a hobby which you would like to tell us about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I-- after I retired-- I was an engineer all my life. After I retired, the company I retired from offered me the services of a psychologist to try to define what I should do with the rest of my life. And I took a whole battery of tests. And after it was all over, they said, gee, you could do anything. You know, you've got-- you've got scores up with Nobel Prize winners. However, there are certain talents you're interested in, and you should either be a hairdresser or painter-- an artist. So I chose being an artist, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a blow-dryer handy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, right. [LAUGHTER] I went back to school, as a matter of fact, Parsons School of Design. And, it's-- along with Klara, this is my new life. And it's like the old life of being an engineer never even existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see that you've actually painted a portrait of Klara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah, I'm rather proud of it. It hangs on our wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to the dance trophies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Klara, do you have anything to say to your husband or to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think we pretty much have said it all. I mean, one could talk endlessly about it. There are many other events, many other incidents. But I think you have a flavor of what my life was during the war. And my happy life, for the most part-- of course, as the saying, to each life, a little rain might have to fall-- we've had our-- some problems. But we've had basically a good marriage, a good life, brought up two healthy children, one adorable grandchild, and are reaping currently the rewards of our hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see that the portrait that Bert has painted of you is making its way toward the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: It looks just like you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to obstruct Klara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like me. [LAUGHS] It's an idealized version of me in my 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is exactly how you look from a-- from a-- from a photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, well, she-- she has been and is a beautiful lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: She thanks you. I thank you, and thank you very much, Klara.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's our pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
